tweets.text:
This is Jensen Huang’s first ever post on X and it’s incredibly bullish for crypto AI

Distributed model training, decentralized inference, open datasets and permissionless compute all become far more important if open-weight models are treated as strategic national infrastructure rather than something to restrict

NVIDIA, Microsoft, Meta and others are arguing that open-weight AI strengthens American leadership because innovation compounds when developers can build on shared foundations instead of closed APIs

Crypto extends that thesis one step further

If open weights democratize access to models, decentralized networks democratize the infrastructure required to train them, serve them and improve them

The endgame isn’t just open AI (no, not chatGPT…)

It’s an open AI economy where compute, data, storage, inference and evaluation are contributed by global networks and coordinated through crypto incentives rather than centralized balance sheets

The paper argues that open-weight AI models are becoming a strategic national asset and that restricting them would weaken, rather than strengthen, US leadership

Crypto AI is simply the infrastructure layer that makes that vision economically scalable

Start paying more attention to networks that enable this open weights architecture to thrive!
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
For my first post, I’m sharing a letter @NVIDIA signed on why open models matter.

AI will transform every industry, power every company, and be built by every country.

Open models strengthen safety and cybersecurity, accelerate innovation and diffusion, and enable sovereignty. https://t.co/t02bi51N4C

tweets.text:
We need more power.

Data center power demand in the US is projected to surge +253% from 2026 levels, to a record 194 gigawatts by 2035.

One gigawatt is roughly equivalent to the capacity of a single traditional nuclear reactor.

As a result, data centers are expected to account for ~20% of US electricity consumption by 2035, up from ~6% today.

To put this into perspective, data centers are estimated to consume ~12% of US electricity by 2030.

Much of this growth is concentrated in a handful of grid regions, such as PJM, which serves the District of Columbia and 13 states, including Virginia, Pennsylvania, and Ohio.

Electricity is the next AI bottleneck.

US equity ETFs have attracted +$880 billion in inflows year-to-date, on track for their largest annual inflow on record.

Investor demand for US equities is unprecedented.

tweets.text:
The AI trade is diverging sharply:

The 30-day correlation between the largest US CapEx spenders and the semiconductor index, $SOX, is down to almost zero, near the lowest in at least 4.5 years.

This marks a sharp decline from the +0.78 positive correlation seen in April.

By comparison, the average correlation coefficient has been +0.60 since the start of 2022.

The divergence comes as semiconductor stocks have rallied while the largest AI infrastructure spenders have declined, and vice versa, since the beginning of June.

This tells us that investors are no longer treating AI infrastructure builders and chipmakers as one trade, as chipmakers benefit from AI demand while hyperscalers face questions over whether massive spending will generate sufficient returns.

The next phase of the AI trade will be defined by profitability, not investment.

tweets.text:
Investors are worried about Oracle's credit:

Oracle's, $ORCL, 5-year credit default swaps (CDS) are up to a record ~203 basis points.

This means protection against Oracle's default now costs ~$203,000 for every $10 million of principal.

Oracle's CDS have more than QUADRUPLED since mid-2025, even surpassing the 2008 Financial Crisis peak.

The company’s borrowing costs are also rising, with the spread on its 6.7% notes due 2056 widening +8 bps on Monday, to 263 bps, and its 5.7% notes due 2036 widening +9 bps, to 205 bps.

Meanwhile, S&P Global Ratings downgraded Oracle to BBB- on July 9th, just 1 notch above junk, citing its rapidly growing AI-related spending.

Oracle's credit risk is at crisis levels.

tweets.text:
I’m starting a new format: Monthly Protocol Reviews.

Let's call them MPR's for short.

These will be a way for me to showcase the best builders in the space.

First up is @SuiNetwork.

Here are all the updates ↓

1. Hashi testnet went live on the 22nd

Hashi lets users borrow against their BTC without moving it off the Bitcoin network.

Collateral is secured by a 2-of-2 multisig, one signature from Hashi's validators and one from a guardian.

25+ partners are building on it ahead of mainnet, and Wave Digital committed to three years of bringing Bitcoin-yield bond products onchain through Hashi.

2. Native SUI staking is live on Coinbase

Rewards accrue directly in the Coinbase account, with no need to choose a validator or manage a separate position.

Sui staking has also been live on Revolut since earlier this year.

This is great for those that cannot deal with the additional hassle from other apps.

3. Sui added confidential transfers (public beta)

Amounts and balances are hidden from public observers, while senders, receivers, and asset issuers keep full visibility.

Exchanges and regulators get risk-tiered access.

No company is moving payroll onchain if competitors can read every payment, so this is a practical solution to that problem.

4. @utila_io added gasless stablecoin transfers

Utila's enterprise users can now send stablecoins on Sui with zero SUI in the wallet.

This is possible due to Sui's gasless stablecoin transfers (added earlier this year).

5. A new record of 6M TPS through Sui Tunnels

In their test run, Sui processed around 2.3B txns in ~20 minutes.

Tunnels handle execution offchain and settle back to Sui, which is how the number gets that high.

It's the largest throughput test the ecosystem has run so far.

6. @0xbeepit added Muse Spark to its Sui trading/prediction agents

Beep says the model outperforms leading models like Opus, GPT, and Gemini on finance tasks.

You can deploy it alongside GPT, Gemini, and other frontier models, and then compare how each performs with your own strategy.

The model is available on both Beep Trading Agent and Predict Agent.

BTC holders, exchange users, and institutions all gained new ways to access and use Sui in July, all within a few weeks.

That's quite an accomplishment for such a short time period.

Hopefully, they continue with this tempo.

Disclosure: I'm a long-time Sui supporter and $SUI holder.

tweets.text:
Our new report "Closing the Loop: The Self-Driving Landscape" is out now!

AI can generate scientific hypotheses faster than laboratories can test them.

Self-driving labs are designed to close that gap by automating the full experimental loop. The model chooses the next experiment, and connected laboratory equipment carries it out. The results feed back into the system and shape what it tests next.

The stakes are especially high in drug discovery. Lead optimization alone can consume roughly three years. Bringing a drug to market takes 10–15 years, with average out-of-pocket and time costs of $2.6 billion per drug.

Self-driving labs target the earlier experimental bottleneck. They could shrink individual cycles from months to days or hours. Running more experiments can also reduce the cost of each run by spreading the upfront cost of automation further.

Every completed experiment adds to a structured record of what worked and what did not. That data improves the model’s next decision, creating a continuous learning loop between AI and the physical lab.

AI has accelerated the generation of scientific hypotheses. Self-driving labs could accelerate the experiments that determine which ideas are worth pursuing.

tweets.full_text:
Our new report "Closing the Loop: The Self-Driving Landscape" is out now!

AI can generate scientific hypotheses faster than laboratories can test them.

Self-driving labs are designed to close that gap by automating the full experimental loop. The model chooses the next experiment, and connected laboratory equipment carries it out. The results feed back into the system and shape what it tests next.

The stakes are especially high in drug discovery. Lead optimization alone can consume roughly three years. Bringing a drug to market takes 10–15 years, with average out-of-pocket and time costs of $2.6 billion per drug.

Self-driving labs target the earlier experimental bottleneck. They could shrink individual cycles from months to days or hours. Running more experiments can also reduce the cost of each run by spreading the upfront cost of automation further.

Every completed experiment adds to a structured record of what worked and what did not. That data improves the model’s next decision, creating a continuous learning loop between AI and the physical lab.

AI has accelerated the generation of scientific hypotheses. Self-driving labs could accelerate the experiments that determine which ideas are worth pursuing.

Read the full report from @accelxr here. First 30 people get it for free.
https://t.co/tcJNxVw02x

tweets.text: On this day in 1959, Vice President Richard Nixon and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev argued about capitalism and communism while standing in a model American kitchen, a confrontation that became known as the Kitchen Debate. The setting was the American National Exhibition in Moscow, where the United States had built a full suburban house stocked with dishwashers, refrigerators, and labor-saving appliances to showcase the consumer abundance of a market economy. Nixon argued that the measure of a system was not its weapons but its ability to put a washing machine and a television in an ordinary worker's home. The exhibition drew nearly 3 million Soviet visitors, many seeing Pepsi, Polaroid cameras, and American cars for the first time. The consumer spending Nixon was selling that day now accounts for roughly 68% of all U.S. GDP.

tweets.text:
Supportive of strong American open-weight models. We're taking action to move more of our work loads to these models at @coinbase.

I think open weight models may actually out-perform frontier models inside companies, because you can fine tune them via reinforcement learning and modify the weights (not just rely on stuffing text into the context window). For example, an open weight and fine tuned model that has seen 100,000 of our compliance cases internally could be a better specialist than any frontier model on its own, which are balanced to be smart across a wide variety of domains.

There is a hybrid approach one could imagine, where an API is provided by a frontier lab or cloud vendor to help you fine tune a frontier model, without revealing the weights publicly. There is still a data sovereignty question here, but at least it would allow fine tuning.

In any event, glad we're seeing more open weight models.

thread_tweets.text:
Seems like a lot of work went into them. Was happy to give them a read. And institutional politics are always present in systems such as this.

In the 2024 BCAP paper I co-wrote with Steve Lee and Ren (which was specifically about contentious soft forks) we highlighted that a key power of the maintainers is the veto: simply by not reviewing or advocating for certain BIPs, they can materially reduce their chances of progressing. And that’s why we highlighted that the prospect for alternative implementations is critical; there has to be away to shift toward other implementations if there is a big enough push to do so. https://t.co/1usS1LD0bN

And that’s an aspect I like about Bitcoin. If I don’t like an implementation, I can run another one.

tweets.text:
Everyone agrees stablecoins are the most obvious use case crypto has ever produced.

Yet I hardly see anyone going for the second and third order effect markets.

If USD stablecoins are that obvious, non-USD stablecoins should be just as obvious. And if that's true, so is the entire FX market sitting behind them.

FX is the single largest market on earth. Roughly $10 trillion in daily volume. And while onchain EUR/USD trades are starting to pick up, they barely exist yet.

Spot DEX volumes hardly ever exceed $30M a day. That's a 333,000x difference. This market essentially doesn't even exist in crypto yet.

And the case for moving it onchain is the same old story:

Instant settlement instead of waiting days, permissionless markets that never close, the same rates for a small business as for a bulge bracket bank and horrendous fees going down to fractions of a cent.

It surely will take time just as USD stablecoins did, but rarely is something this obvious also this big.

tweets.text:
$Meta is building this data center from scratch on ~1,000 acres of land land it bought from the City of El Paso in 2023 for $9M. 
El Paso Electric has about 2.9 GW of total generation capacity serving ~460K customers, so a 1 GW Meta campus is nearly the size of the entire existing utility system, which is why they're building dedicated gas generation. 
The site selection logic appears to be cheap land, proximity to Meta's existing campuses in New Mexico and Arizona (Los Lunas, Mesa), and the ability to negotiate custom power arrangements with a smaller regional utility rather than fighting ERCOT interconnection queues in the Dallas/Austin corridor.

tweets.text:
Bitmex shutting down is truly an end of an era — but it had a very long, and sadly predictable windup. 

The story of why this shutdown happens, instead of an orderly sale that would preserve a storied brand and the jobs of probably dozens of employees, was seeded years ago. It goes back to a blog post I wrote all the way back in 2018 and that was read 50k times (crazy how time flies):

https://t.co/Os78hvZnrg

I wrote back then:

"If BitMEX closes a position in time, often some change from the margin is left over. This money goes into an insurance fund."

and

"Between 2016 and today, it has grown steadily to more than 14.000 Bitcoin (almost $100M at [2018]s prices!), 11.000 of which were added in 2018."

Today, it should be around $270m. However, the size was never as much the problem as the structure, and the poor incentives it would create.

"First, the money is not stored in a segregated account. Second, BitMEX doesn’t say how large they intend it to grow, or what will happen to the excess Bitcoins in it. This indicates to me that they view the fund as another asset on their balance sheet, that they can grow as much as possible and eventually liquidate."

In effect, I was arguing that Bitmex's poorly structured insurance fund incentivized them to do three things: (1) liquidate customers more aggressively than necessary, (2) monetize those customers via the unnecessary liquidations, and (3) eventually liquidate the asset itself — quite possibly in a way misaligned with every other stakeholder of the exchange.

It's worth remembering that in 2018, Bitmex had a quasi-monopoly on crypto perps. That became their trap: when you're quietly printing money at that scale, you're never really incentivized to fix the thing generating it. So Bitmex never seriously improved how it liquidated users — and got outcompeted on product by FTX, whose partial-liquidation engine was simply the better design for the traders who cared most about not being needlessly wiped out.

Which finally brings us to why this is a shutdown and not a sale. We now know Bitmex was looking for a buyer since at least 02/25, but couldn't find a deal it was willing to take. All at a time when many other crypto exchanges found partnerships and investment from top tradfi players. 

I think the insurance fund is how we square that circle. My guess is that the insurance fund was such a legally toxic asset that nobody would touch it. Does this money belong to an acquirer, or the customers it was skimmed from? It's hard for a buyer to inherit that question, at least at a price that the Bitmex founders knew they could generate by simply walking away, and probably cashing out the insurance fund for themselves. 

So that's how it ends. Bitmex built a very profitable but misaligned business model around its insurance fund — one that left it exposed to a counter-positioned competitor and, in the end, made the business impossible to sell. It started as the golden goose, and then became the noose.

Technically, the deal has not officially collapsed yet. But in reality, it is over. The Prime Minister and the Minister of Defense have already made it clear that the transaction will not be approved for national security reasons.

The fact that a company was willing to pay around $35 per share, with the possibility that other bidders could have offered even more, says a lot about $ZIM ’s value.

When an acquirer is willing to pay that kind of price, it’s usually because they believe the business is worth significantly more than what they are paying, often 50% or more above the purchase price. It gives you an idea of the underlying value they saw in ZIM.

tweets.text:
Most of the market is priced for a world that's coming to an end.

Traditional software, legacy platforms, much of the SaaS layer of the S&P 500… all built on the assumption that humans will remain the primary customers.

AI agents don't buy software the way we do. They don't need dashboards, subscriptions, or logins. And once the buyer changes, so does everything built around them.

The investable opportunity ahead is the agentic economy… what gets built for agents as customers.

That's where the next wave of capital ends up, and most people aren't positioned for it.

tweets.text: In typical organizations, most decisions are made either autocratically, by a top-down leader, or democratically, where everyone shares their opinions and those opinions that have the most support are implemented. Both systems produce inferior decision making. That’s because the best decisions are made by an idea meritocracy with believability-weighted decision making, in which the most capable people work through their disagreements with other capable people who have thought independently about what is true and what to do about it. #principleoftheday

tweets.text:
People say AI is expensive.

Let me break it down. I pay $200 a month for Claude and get PhD-level intelligence on every subject known to humanity.

Now price a human. Globally, on average, maybe $10,000 a year in food to run one brain. Plus the twenty odd years it takes to grow it first.

So it's $10,000 a year for human-level compute, versus $200 a month for intelligence that clears any human on any topic.

That's why it's scaling this fast. The price of human intelligence is just uneconomical compared to machines.

I’m increasingly convinced that:

Over the next five years, chains that prioritise security-first neutrality and focus on building objective/publicly verifiable defense against hacks will outperform those that don’t.

Chains can do this and still remain a Stage 1 rollup is very useful.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
How to and how not to censor an attacker on a Stage 1 rollup (based on our experience in Security Councils) 🧵

tl;dr: our recommendation is for emergency actions to be initiated by the operator itself. This won't affect the Stage as long as the pause is automatically expiring
